docs.title: CLI Agent Coding Guide (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, etc.)
docs.text: Goal: Turn AI IDEs and CLI agents into reliable “shoot-and-forget” helpers for real projects, using patterns inspired by Claude Code’s feature set.

docs.title: 1. Core Philosophy
docs.text:
1. Treat the agent as a teammate that owns a task end-to-end. Judge it by the final PR, not the vibe of the chat.:contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}
  2. Keep a small, curated “constitution” file in the repo (like CLAUDE.md) with guardrails and pointers, not a full manual.:contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}
  3. Prefer simple CLIs and scripts over complex, brittle tool graphs. Let the agent write glue code and scripts as needed.:contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}
  4. Use logs, settings, and GH Actions as feedback loops to refine your tools and docs over time.:contentReference[oaicite:3]{index=3}

docs.title: 2. Project “Constitution” (CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md)
docs.text: Use a single root file as the primary contract between your repo and any coding agent.

docs.title: 2.1 Purpose
docs.text:
1. Define how this repo works at a high level.
  2. Document only the tools and flows that many engineers actually use (for example, ≥30%).:contentReference[oaicite:4]{index=4}
  3. Act as a forcing function: if a tool needs a page of explanation, fix the tool (simplify the CLI) instead of bloating the doc.:contentReference[oaicite:5]{index=5}

docs.title: 2.2 Content Guidelines
docs.text:
1. Start with guardrails and recurring mistakes, not an exhaustive manual.:contentReference[oaicite:6]{index=6}
  2. Don’t @-embed large docs into this file. Instead:
  3. Explain when to read a specific doc.
  4. Link it by path and describe the trigger (for example, “On FooBarError, see docs/foo/troubleshooting.md.").:contentReference[oaicite:7]{index=7}
  5. Avoid bare “never do X” rules. Always pair them with a recommended alternative (for example, “Never use --foo; use baz run instead.”).:contentReference[oaicite:8]{index=8}
  6. Mirror this file to AGENTS.md (or similar) so other AI IDEs can reuse the same guidance.:contentReference[oaicite:9]{index=9}

docs.title: 3. Context Management Workflows
docs.text: Use explicit workflows instead of trusting opaque auto-compaction.

docs.title: 3.1 Inspect Context
docs.text:
1. Run the tool’s “context inspector” (for Claude: /context) mid-session to see where the context window is going.:contentReference[oaicite:10]{index=10}
  2. Treat context like disk space: you have a baseline cost for repo metadata, then working space for your change.

docs.title: 3.2 Simple Restart: Clear and Catch Up
docs.text:
1. Use a “clear” command to wipe chat history (for Claude: /clear).:contentReference[oaicite:11]{index=11}
  2. Immediately run a “catch up” command that:
  3. Reads all files changed in your current branch.
  4. Re-establishes what you are working on.:contentReference[oaicite:12]{index=12}

docs.title: 3.3 Document & Clear (For Large Tasks)
docs.text:
1. Ask the agent to dump its current plan and progress into a .md file.
  2. Clear the session.
  3. Start a new session by:
  4. Having it read the summary .md.
  5. Continuing from there.:contentReference[oaicite:13]{index=13}

Avoid auto-compaction if you can; keep resets explicit and documented.

docs.title: 4.1 Custom Slash Commands
docs.text:
Use them as shortcuts, not a second programming language.

  1. Keep a minimal set of commands (for example, /catchup, /pr).:contentReference[oaicite:14]{index=14}
  2. Avoid long, complex command catalogs that users have to memorize. If you need a lot of ceremony, your core flows and docs probably need to be simplified.:contentReference[oaicite:15]{index=15}

docs.title: 4.2 Subagents vs. Clones
docs.text:
Be careful when hiding context behind specialized subagents.

  1. Subagents can gatekeep context (tests, deploy instructions) from the main agent. It now has to call a subagent just to understand how to validate its own work.:contentReference[oaicite:16]{index=16}
  2. Subagents hard-code human workflows; you’re telling the agent exactly how to delegate, which reduces flexibility.:contentReference[oaicite:17]{index=17}
  3. Prefer “master-clone” style:
  4. Put all key context into CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md.
  5. Let the main agent spawn clones (for Claude: Task(...) / Explore(...)) to parallelize work.:contentReference[oaicite:18]{index=18}

docs.title: 5.1 Resume & History
docs.text:
1. Use resume/continue features to:
  2. Reboot a crashed session.
  3. Ask “what did we do here?” days later.:contentReference[oaicite:19]{index=19}
  4. Periodically analyze raw session logs for:
  5. Common exceptions.
  6. Repeated permission prompts.
  7. Error patterns that should be fixed in tools or docs.:contentReference[oaicite:20]{index=20}

docs.title: 5.2 Hooks (Guardrails at Commit Time)
docs.text:
1. Use hooks as deterministic rules that complement the softer guidance in CLAUDE.md.:contentReference[oaicite:21]{index=21}
  2. Prefer block-at-submit hooks:
  3. Wrap git commit (or equivalent) with a hook that:
    • Runs tests or checks.
    • Only allows commit after everything passes.:contentReference[oaicite:22]{index=22}
  4. Use hint hooks to give non-blocking feedback for suboptimal behavior.:contentReference[oaicite:23]{index=23}
  5. Avoid blocking mid-edit. Let the agent finish its plan, then validate the final diff at commit time.:contentReference[oaicite:24]{index=24}

docs.title: 5.3 Planning Mode
docs.text:
1. Always use planning mode (or equivalent) for large changes.:contentReference[oaicite:25]{index=25}
  2. For small projects, rely on the built-in planner to:
  3. Agree on a design.
  4. Define checkpoints where the agent must stop and show progress.:contentReference[oaicite:26]{index=26}
  5. For bigger orgs, consider a custom planning tool that:
  6. Outputs plans in your internal design format.
  7. Bakes in privacy, security, and architectural best practices.:contentReference[oaicite:27]{index=27}

docs.title: 6.1 Skills as Scripting Abstractions
docs.text:
1. Think in three layers of autonomy:
  2. Single Prompt – all context in one mega prompt (brittle).
  3. Tool Calling – many small tools that mimic APIs (better, but can be rigid).
  4. Scripting – give access to binaries/scripts; agent writes code to orchestrate them.:contentReference[oaicite:28]{index=28}
  5. Skills are a clean way to package the scripting layer: a discoverable catalog of CLIs and scripts, documented in something like SKILL.md.:contentReference[oaicite:29]{index=29}

docs.title: 6.2 MCP as a Secure Gateway
docs.text:
1. Don’t mirror full REST APIs into MCP with dozens of near-duplicate tools.:contentReference[oaicite:30]{index=30}
  2. Use MCP for what actually needs a secure boundary:
  3. Download raw or sensitive data.
  4. Perform gated actions.
  5. Run code inside a controlled environment.:contentReference[oaicite:31]{index=31}
  6. Keep MCP tools few and high-level; let scripting and skills handle the rest of the workflow.:contentReference[oaicite:32]{index=32}

docs.title: 7.1 SDK as an Agent Framework
docs.text:
Use the agent SDK (for example, Claude Code SDK) when you need more than interactive chat.

  1. Batch / parallel edits:
  2. Write scripts that call the CLI with focused prompts (for example, “in /pathA, change all refs from foo to bar”), running multiple tasks in parallel.:contentReference[oaicite:33]{index=33}
  3. Internal chat tools:
  4. Wrap complex processes in simple chat UIs for non-technical users.
  5. Use the SDK to handle “try normal installer; on error, let the agent fix it”.:contentReference[oaicite:34]{index=34}
  6. Rapid agent prototyping:
  7. Prototype new agents (for security, data analysis, ops, etc.) with the SDK before committing to a full platform.:contentReference[oaicite:35]{index=35}

docs.title: 7.2 GitHub Action Integration
docs.text:
1. Run the agent in CI via a GitHub Action:
  2. Full control over container and environment.
  3. Strong sandboxing and auditability.:contentReference[oaicite:36]{index=36}
  4. Build “PR-from-anywhere” flows:
  5. Trigger via Slack, Jira, alerts, etc.
  6. Let the GHA run the agent to produce a tested PR.:contentReference[oaicite:37]{index=37}
  7. Mine GHA logs:
  8. Regularly query agent logs for recurring mistakes.
  9. Feed insights back into CLAUDE.md, skills, and CLIs.:contentReference[oaicite:38]{index=38}

docs.title: 8. Settings and Advanced Tuning
docs.text:
Maintain a settings.json (or equivalent) for agent and network behavior.

  1. Proxies:
  2. Use HTTPS_PROXY / HTTP_PROXY for debugging raw traffic or sandboxing background agents.:contentReference[oaicite:39]{index=39}
  3. Timeouts:
  4. Increase tool/bash timeouts if you routinely run long commands.:contentReference[oaicite:40]{index=40}
  5. API Keys:
  6. Prefer centralized, usage-based API keys (enterprise-style) over per-seat keys when possible.:contentReference[oaicite:41]{index=41}
  7. Permissions:
  8. Periodically audit the list of commands the agent may auto-run. Tighten or expand as workflows evolve.:contentReference[oaicite:42]{index=42}

docs.title: 9. Quick Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] Root-level “constitution” file (CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md) with guardrails and main flows.
  • [ ] Clear context workflows: inspect, clear, catch up, document & clear.
  • [ ] Minimal, meaningful slash commands; no giant command catalogs.
  • [ ] Hooks that enforce testing and checks at commit time, not mid-edit.
  • [ ] Planning mode (or equivalent) used for all large changes.
  • [ ] Skills and CLIs documented as a scripting layer; MCP used only as a secure data/action gateway.
  • [ ] SDK and GH Actions integrated for batch edits, internal tools, and PR automation.
  • [ ] settings.json (or equivalent) maintained for proxies, timeouts, keys, and permissions.

docs.title: Markdown Fetcher Tool
docs.text: The Markdown Fetcher is a utility for fetching web content and converting it to clean Markdown using markdown.new. This is particularly useful for AI agents that need structured data from web pages.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The markdown_fetcher module provides a 3-tier fallback strategy for converting web content to Markdown:

  1. Tier 1 - Native Method: Tries to fetch with Accept: text/markdown header (fastest)
  2. Tier 2 - Workers AI: Uses Cloudflare Workers AI for HTML-to-Markdown conversion
  3. Tier 3 - Browser Rendering: Uses Cloudflare Browser Rendering for JS-heavy pages

This approach provides up to 80% token reduction compared to raw HTML.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
The markdown_fetcher utility is included in this repository's current scaffold. Ensure dependencies are installed:

uv sync

The tool requires requests>=2.31.0, which is already included in the current project dependencies.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
from vibe_coding.utils.markdown_fetcher import fetch_markdown

result = fetch_markdown("https://example.com")
print(result.content)
print(f"Tokens: {result.metadata.token_count}")
print(f"Method: {result.metadata.method_used}")

docs.title: Custom Configuration
docs.text:
from vibe_coding.utils.markdown_fetcher import fetch_markdown, MarkdownFetcherConfig

config = MarkdownFetcherConfig(
    method="ai",           # Use only Workers AI
    retain_images=True,     # Keep images in output
    timeout=60,            # 60 second timeout
    max_retries=5,        # Retry up to 5 times
    backoff_factor=3.0,    # Exponential backoff multiplier
)

result = fetch_markdown("https://example.com", config)

docs.title: Configuration Options
docs.text: Parameter Type Default Description method "auto", "ai", "browser" "auto" Conversion method to use. "auto" tries all tiers. retain_images bool False Whether to include images in the output. timeout int 30 Request timeout in seconds. Browser rendering uses minimum 60s. max_retries int 3 Maximum number of retry attempts for failed requests. backoff_factor float 2.0 Exponential backoff multiplier for retries.

docs.title: Method Selection
docs.text:
• auto (default): Tries all three tiers in order
  • Best for general use
  • Fastest possible with fallbacks

  • Automatic handling of different page types

  • ai: Only uses Workers AI

  • Good when you know the site doesn't support native markdown
  • Faster than browser rendering

  • May not work with heavily JavaScript-based pages

  • browser: Only uses Browser Rendering

  • Best for JS-heavy single-page applications
  • Slowest but most reliable for complex pages
  • Minimum 60-second timeout

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
The tool provides specific exception types for different failure scenarios:

from vibe_coding.utils.markdown_fetcher import (
    fetch_markdown,
    MarkdownFetchError,
    MarkdownTimeoutError,
    MarkdownRateLimitError,
    MarkdownValidationError,
)

try:
    result = fetch_markdown("https://example.com")
except MarkdownValidationError as e:
    print(f"Invalid URL: {e}")
except MarkdownTimeoutError as e:
    print(f"Request timed out: {e}")
except MarkdownRateLimitError as e:
    print(f"Rate limit exceeded: {e}")
except MarkdownFetchError as e:
    print(f"Fetch failed: {e}")

docs.title: Exception Types
docs.text: Exception When Raised Typical Cause MarkdownValidationError URL is malformed or uses unsupported scheme Invalid URL, file://, javascript:, data: MarkdownTimeoutError Request exceeds timeout Slow server, large page, network issues MarkdownRateLimitError markdown.new rate limit exceeded Too many requests in short period MarkdownFetchError All retries exhausted Persistent network/server errors

docs.title: Example 1: Fetch a Blog Post
docs.text:
from vibe_coding.utils.markdown_fetcher import fetch_markdown

url = "https://blog.example.com/post/interesting-article"
result = fetch_markdown(url)

print(f"Content ({result.metadata.token_count} tokens):")
print(result.content[:500] + "...")

docs.title: Example 2: Fetch Documentation with Images
docs.text:
from vibe_coding.utils.markdown_fetcher import fetch_markdown, MarkdownFetcherConfig

config = MarkdownFetcherConfig(retain_images=True)
result = fetch_markdown("https://docs.example.com", config)

print(f"Fetched using {result.metadata.method_used} method")
print(f"Response time: {result.metadata.response_time_ms:.0f}ms")

docs.title: Example 3: Retry Aggressive Retry for Unstable Sites
docs.text:
from vibe_coding.utils.markdown_fetcher import fetch_markdown, MarkdownFetcherConfig

config = MarkdownFetcherConfig(
    max_retries=10,        # Try up to 10 times
    backoff_factor=1.5,      # Slower backoff
    timeout=90,              # Longer timeout
)

result = fetch_markdown("https://unstable.example.com", config)

docs.title: Example 4: Browser Rendering for SPA
docs.text:
from vibe_coding.utils.markdown_fetcher import fetch_markdown, MarkdownFetcherConfig

config = MarkdownFetcherConfig(
    method="browser",     # Force browser rendering
    timeout=120,           # 2 minute timeout for complex pages
)

result = fetch_markdown("https://react-app.example.com", config)

docs.title: Accessing Metadata
docs.text:
result = fetch_markdown("https://example.com")

metadata = result.metadata
print(f"Tokens: {metadata.token_count}")
print(f"Method: {metadata.method_used}")
print(f"Status: {metadata.status_code}")
print(f"Response time: {metadata.response_time_ms:.0f}ms")

docs.title: Custom Retry Strategy
docs.text:
For rate-limited APIs or unstable sites, configure aggressive retry:

config = MarkdownFetcherConfig(
    max_retries=5,        # More retries
    backoff_factor=4.0,      # Faster backoff growth
)

result = fetch_markdown("https://rate-limited.com", config)

docs.title: Token Efficiency
docs.text:
• Native markdown: Fastest, no conversion needed
  • Workers AI: ~100-200ms additional latency
  • Browser Rendering: ~1-3s additional latency

Choose the method that balances speed and accuracy for your use case.

docs.title: Timeout Guidelines
docs.text: Page Type Recommended Method Timeout Static HTML auto or ai 30s Dynamic with some JS auto 45s Heavy SPA/React/Vue browser 90-120s

docs.title: "Rate limit exceeded" errors
docs.text:
Problem: Receiving MarkdownRateLimitError

Solutions: 1. Increase backoff_factor for longer delays between retries 2. Add delays between fetches in your application 3. Implement request queuing or caching

docs.title: Timeout errors
docs.text:
Problem: MarkdownTimeoutError on complex pages

Solutions: 1. Increase timeout value (especially for browser method) 2. Switch to browser method for JS-heavy pages 3. Check network connectivity

docs.title: SSL/Certificate errors
docs.text:
Problem: SSL verification failures

Solutions: 1. The tool uses system certificates - ensure they're up to date 2. For development only, you can disable verification (not recommended for production)

docs.title: Empty or incorrect content
docs.text:
Problem: Content doesn't match expected page

Solutions: 1. Check if the page requires JavaScript - use method="browser" 2. Verify the URL is correct and publicly accessible 3. Check result.metadata.method_used to see which tier succeeded

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
The module includes comprehensive tests with 95% coverage:

# Run all markdown fetcher tests
uv run pytest tests/utils/test_markdown_fetcher.py -v

# Run with coverage
uv run pytest tests/utils/test_markdown_fetcher.py --cov=vibe_coding.utils.markdown_fetcher

docs.title: Architecture Decision
docs.text: See ADR-001: Markdown.new Integration for the decision to use markdown.new as an external-only dependency.

docs.title: Related Tools
docs.text:
• Web Scraping: For more complex scraping needs, consider scrapy or beautifulsoup4
  • HTML Parsing: The tool handles HTML-to-Markdown conversion automatically
  • Caching: Consider adding local caching for frequently accessed URLs

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
1. External Dependency: Requires internet connectivity to markdown.new
  2. Rate Limits: markdown.new may rate limit excessive requests
  3. JavaScript: Only browser method fully handles client-side rendering
  4. Authentication: Does not currently support authenticated requests
  5. Cookies/Sessions: Does not maintain session state across requests

docs.title: Future Enhancements
docs.text:
Potential improvements for future versions:

  • [ ] Local fallback using html2text or similar
  • [ ] Request caching to avoid repeated fetches
  • [ ] Support for authenticated requests
  • [ ] Session management for cookie-based auth
  • [ ] Batch fetching multiple URLs
  • [ ] Custom headers support for API keys

docs.text:

docs.title: Pre-Commit Hooks
docs.text: This document describes the pre-commit hooks configured for this project and how to use them.

docs.title: What Are Pre-Commit Hooks?
docs.text: Pre-commit hooks are automated checks that run before you create a git commit. They help ensure code quality, security, and consistency by catching issues early in the development process.

docs.title: First-Time Setup
docs.text:
# Install pre-commit hooks (run once)
pre-commit install

# Verify installation
pre-commit --version

Note: If you don't have pre-commit installed globally, you can add it as a dev dependency:

uv add --dev pre-commit

docs.title: Configured Hooks
docs.text: The .pre-commit-config.yaml file defines the following checks:

docs.title: 1. ruff (Linting & Formatting)
docs.text:
• Purpose: Fast Python linter and code formatter
  • What it checks:
  • Code style issues
  • Import sorting
  • Common programming errors
  • Auto-fixes: Most issues can be auto-fixed with --fix

docs.title: 2. bandit (Security)
docs.text:
• Purpose: Find common security issues in Python code
  • What it checks:
  • Hardcoded passwords
  • Insecure cryptographic functions
  • SQL injection vulnerabilities
  • And more...

docs.title: 3. safety (Dependency Security)
docs.text:
• Purpose: Check for known security vulnerabilities in dependencies
  • What it checks:
  • Python packages with CVEs
  • Outdated dependencies with known issues

docs.title: Automatic (Recommended)
docs.text:
Once installed, hooks run automatically when you run git commit:

git add .
git commit -m "feat: add new feature"
# Hooks run automatically here

docs.title: Manual Run
docs.text:
You can run hooks manually on all files:

# Run all hooks on all files
pre-commit run --all-files

# Run specific hook
pre-commit run ruff --all-files

docs.title: Running on Staged Files Only
docs.text: pre-commit run

docs.title: Scenario 1: Hook Failed but Auto-Fixed Issues
docs.text:
If a hook failed but auto-fixed some issues:

# Stage the auto-fixed files
git add <fixed-files>

# Try committing again
git commit -m "feat: add new feature"

docs.title: Scenario 2: Hook Failed - Manual Fix Required
docs.text:
If a hook failed and requires manual intervention:

# Read the error message carefully
# Fix the issues
# Stage changes
git add .
git commit -m "feat: add new feature"

docs.title: Scenario 3: Skip Hooks (Not Recommended)
docs.text:
⚠️ WARNING: Only skip hooks in emergencies or when you know what you're doing.

git commit --no-verify -m "feat: add new feature"

docs.title: Scenario 4: Update Hooks
docs.text:
Update hook versions to latest:

pre-commit autoupdate

docs.title: Hook is Slow
docs.text:
If hooks are taking too long:

  1. Run specific hooks only: pre-commit run ruff
  2. Check if uv dependencies are cached
  3. Consider running hooks only on changed files: pre-commit run

docs.title: Hook Not Found
docs.text:
If you get "hook not found" errors:

# Reinstall hooks
pre-commit install --force

# Or clean and reinstall
rm -rf ~/.cache/pre-commit
pre-commit install

docs.title: Import Errors in Hooks
docs.text:
If hooks report import errors:

# Sync dependencies
uv sync

# Reinstall hooks
pre-commit clean
pre-commit install

docs.title: Adding New Hooks
docs.text:
Edit .pre-commit-config.yaml:

- repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
  rev: v4.5.0
  hooks:
    - id: trailing-whitespace
    - id: end-of-file-fixer
    - id: check-yaml

docs.title: Disabling Hooks Temporarily
docs.text:
In a specific commit message:

git commit -m "feat: add new feature

skip-checks: true"

Or use the --no-verify flag (emergency only).

docs.title: CI Integration
docs.text:
Pre-commit hooks are also run in CI to ensure consistency:

# In GitHub Actions
- name: Run pre-commit
  run: pre-commit run --all-files

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Always install hooks on a new clone
  2. Don't skip hooks unless absolutely necessary
  3. Fix issues immediately when hooks fail
  4. Keep hooks updated with pre-commit autoupdate
  5. Document custom hooks in this file

docs.title: Quick Reference
docs.text:
Command Description pre-commit install Install hooks in this repo pre-commit run Run on staged files pre-commit run --all-files Run on all files pre-commit run <hook-id> Run specific hook pre-commit autoupdate Update hook versions pre-commit clean Clean hook cache git commit --no-verify Skip hooks (emergency)
